coredata.dc:title: Bound states of Dirac electrons in a graphene-based magnetic quantum dot
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2009 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dali
Jin, Guojun
coredata.dc:description: We investigate the magnetically confined states of the massless Dirac fermions in a graphene quantum dot formed by the inhomogeneous distributions of the magnetic fields inside and outside the dot. The calculated energy spectrum exhibits quite different features with and without the magnetic field inside the dot. It is found that the degeneracy of the relativistic Landau level with negative angular momenta can be lifted, and this degeneracy breaking can be modulated by the magnetic field inside the dot. Moreover, such a system can form the strongly localized states within the dot and along its boundary, especially with the magnetic field inside the dot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dirac electrons
Graphene quantum dot
Magnetic confinement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(Color online.) Energies of the eignenstates (n,m) with m ⩽0 versus the missing flux δ at a given outer magnetic field B =4 T for the two different cases of the inner magnetic fields: (a)
B i
= 0 and (b)
B i
= 2 T .

(Color online.) (a) Energies of the eigenstates (n,m) with m ⩽0 as a function of the inner magnetic field
B i
(
B i
> 0 ) at B =4 T and R =50 nm. (b) Dependence of some low-lying energy levels on the direction of the magnetic fields inside and outside the dot. Here, the inner magnetic field
B i
changes from −6 T to 6 T at B =4 T and R =50 nm.

(Color online.) Dependence of the eigenenergy of the GQD on angular quantum number m at B =4 T and R =50 nm for the two different cases of the inner magnetic fields: (a)
B i
= 0 and (b)
B i
= 2 T . Dotted lines, respectively, indicate the relativistic Landau levels with n =0,1,2.

(Color online.) Probability density of the Dirac electron in graphene at B =20 T for the two cases of
B i
= 0 (solid line) and
B i
= 10 T (dashed line). Three different eigenstates are considered: (a) (0,0), (b) (0,−1) and (c) (0,1). The insets in each panel show the probability density distributions in graphene plane, where the left is for
B i
= 0 and the right is for
B i
= 10 T . The dotted circle in each inset represents the radius of the GQD, and the different gray scales indicate the probability densities in various regions.

head.ce:title: Bound states of Dirac electrons in a graphene-based magnetic quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures and Department of Physics, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigate the magnetically confined states of the massless Dirac fermions in a graphene quantum dot formed by the inhomogeneous distributions of the magnetic fields inside and outside the dot. The calculated energy spectrum exhibits quite different features with and without the magnetic field inside the dot. It is found that the degeneracy of the relativistic Landau level with negative angular momenta can be lifted, and this degeneracy breaking can be modulated by the magnetic field inside the dot. Moreover, such a system can form the strongly localized states within the dot and along its boundary, especially with the magnetic field inside the dot.

ce:sections.ce:para:
Since the successful preparation of monolayer graphene films [1], they have stimulated a great deal of research and exhibited many unusual properties [2–5]. Among these striking properties, the relativistic-like behavior and the zero effective mass result from the unique band structure of graphene, i.e. the gapless and approximately linear energy dispersion relation near the Fermi energy at the vicinity of the K and
K ′
valleys in the Brillouin zone. Thus the charged carriers in undoped graphene can be described as the massless relativistic fermions and are governed by the two-dimensional Dirac equation.

However, due to the Klein tunneling [6,7], Dirac electrons in graphene cannot be efficiently confined within finite spatial areas by the usual way. This effect is disadvantageous for the creation of useful mesoscopic structures in graphene. Some theoretical attempts have been suggested to trap Dirac electrons in a graphene quantum dot and to form quasibound states by using electrostatic potentials [8–10]. Recently, another alternative approaches have been proposed by means of inhomogeneous magnetic fields [11–14]. On the other hand, the inhomogeneous magnetic fields have been realized by using nanostructured ferromagnetic materials [15–18] or depositing a thin superconducting film on top of a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) [19–21], and have already been successfully used to produce various magnetic quantum structures in the 2DEG, e.g. magnetic quantum step, barrier, well, and magnetic billiard. Theoretical developments have focused on the physical properties of the Schrodinger fermions confined in these magnetic quantum structures [22–27].

Up to now two types of the inhomogeneous magnetic fields have been used to create the graphene quantum dot (GQD). One is that the magnetic field is zero inside a circular region and nonzero outside it [12]. The other is that the magnetic field is nonzero only in a circle and zero elsewhere [14]. In this Letter we propose a different model system in which the magnetic fields inside and outside the circular dot are all present, and their directions are perpendicular to the graphene plane. Such a magnetic structure can be realized by the combination of a ferromagnetic disk with a homogeneous magnetic field [28]. Also, noting that the sharp-edge form of step magnetic field profiles is suitable when the Fermi length
λ F
is parametrically larger than the edge smearing length
λ s
in real systems, and that
λ s
≫ a (lattice constant a) to ensure the smoothness of the magnetic field [12].

As we have known, the electrons near the Fermi level in graphene obey the massless relativistic Dirac equation. Because of the valley degeneracy in the low-energy regime, one can only consider one of the two inequivalent valleys in the Brillouin zone. Also, a slowly varying magnetic field on the scale of the lattice constant do not couple the two valleys [29–31]. Therefore, the Hamiltonian for our system can be written as (with ℏ = c = 1 )
(1)
H =
v F
( − i ∇ + e A ) ⋅ σ ,
where
v F
≈
10 6
m / s is the Fermi velocity, e is the absolute value of the electron charge, A is the vector potential, and σ = (
σ x
,
σ y
) is the 2 × 2 Pauli matrices vector. Considering the cylindrical symmetry of our system, in the cylindrical coordinates, the above Hamiltonian is further expressed as
(2)
H ( r , φ ) = − i
v F
(
0
e
− i φ
e
i φ
0
)
∂
∂ r
+
v F
(
0
−
e
− i φ
e
i φ
0
)
1 r
(
∂
∂ φ
+ i
Φ
Φ 0
)
,
where Φ is the magnetic flux passing through the selected regions and
Φ 0
= 2 π / e .

Since H commutes with the total angular momentum operator
J z
= − i
∂
∂ φ
+
1 2
σ z
, the energy eigenspinors can be chosen as
(3)
ψ ( r , φ ) =
(
χ A
( r )
e
i m φ
i
χ B
( r )
e
i ( m + 1 ) φ
)
,
with total angular momentum quantum number m being an integer [32].
χ A
( r ) and
χ B
( r ) are the radial parts of the spinor wavefunction corresponding to the two sublattice atoms, respectively. Substituting Eqs. (2) and (3) into the two-dimensional Dirac equation, we arrive at a set of two coupled differential equations for the radial wavefunction components,
(4a)
[
d
d r
+
1 r
( m +
Φ
Φ 0
+ 1 )
]
χ B
( r ) = k
χ A
( r ) ,
(4b)
−
[
d
d r
−
1 r
( m +
Φ
Φ 0
)
]
χ A
( r ) = k
χ B
( r ) ,
where k = E /
v F
is the wave vector. Instead of solving directly Eq. (4), it is more convenient to convert them into second-order differential equation for a certain component, e.g.
χ A
( r ) ,
(5)
{
d 2
d
r 2
+
1 r
d
d r
+
[
k 2
−
V ˜
( r ) ]
}
χ A
( r ) = 0 ,
which has to be solved in the two regions, i.e. r < R and r > R . Here the reduced confining potential
V ˜
( r ) is expressed divisionally as
(6)
V ˜
( r ) =
{
(
m r
+
e
B i
r
2
)
2
+ e
B i
( r < R ) ,
(
m ˜
1
r
+
e B r
2
)
2
+ e B
( r > R ) ,
where R is the radius of the GQD,
B i
and B are the magnetic fields inside and outside the dot, and
m ˜
1
= m − δ + e
B i
R 2
/ 2 is the modified angular quantum number with δ = e B
R 2
/ 2 being the reduced missing flux.

The solution of Eq. (5) is derived analytically as
(7)
χ A
( r ) =
{
C 1
r
| m |
e
− e
B i
r 2
/ 4
L (
a 1
, | m | ,
e
B i
r 2
2
)
( r < R ) ,
C 2

|

|

− e B
r 2
/ 4
U (
a 2
, |
m ˜
1
| + 1 ,
e B
r 2
2
)
( r > R ) .
In the case of k ≠ 0 ,
χ B
( r ) can follow from
χ A
( r ) via Eq. (4),
(8)
χ B
( r ) =
{
C 1
r
| m | − 1
e
−
e
B i
r 2
4
e
B i
k
{
r 2
L (
a 1
− 1 , | m | + 1 ,
e
B i
r 2
2
)
+ [
r 2
+
( m − | m | )
e
B i
] L (
a 1
, | m | ,
e
B i
r 2
2
) }
( r < R ) ,
C 2
r
|
m ˜
1
| − 1
e
−
e B
r 2
4
e B
k
{
a 2
r 2
U (
a 2
+ 1 , |
m ˜
1
| + 2 ,
e B
r 2
2
)
+ [
r 2
+
(
m ˜
1
− |
m ˜
1
| )
e B
] U (
a 2
, |
m ˜
1
| + 1 ,
e B
r 2
2
) }
( r > R ) .
In Eqs. (7) and (8),
a 1
=
1 2
(
k 2
e
B i
− 2 − m − | m | ) and
a 2
=
1 2
( 2 +
m ˜
1
+ |
m ˜
1
| −
k 2
e B
) . L ( a , b , x ) is the generalized Laguerre polynomial, and U ( a , b , x ) is the confluent hypergeometric function of the second kind.
C 1
and
C 2
are the normalization constants. Applying the continuity of the eigenspinor wavefunction ψ ( r , φ ) at the dot border R, we finally obtain an equation for determining the wave vector k. This equation can be solved using the bracketing and bisection numerical technique, and there is an infinite number of roots for given m, R, B and
B i
which fulfill the equation. Since the energy spectrum is electron-hole symmetric, we here only discuss the positive energy corresponding to the electron-like states and define the radial quantum number n which labels the positive roots of the equation in turn.

Based on the above formulations, we study the magnetic confinement of the Dirac electron in the GQD induced by the inhomogeneous magnetic fields. Fig. 1 shows the variation of the low-energy eigenenergies with the missing flux δ at B = 4 T for the two different cases of the inner magnetic fields
B i
. It is well known that the relativistic Landau levels for graphene are given by
E
n m
=
v F
e B [ 2 ( n + 1 ) + | m | + m ]
( n = 0 , 1 , 2 , … ) . Obviously, for a fixed n, all states with negative angular quantum number m are degenerate. However, for the GQD considered here, the degeneracies of the eigenstates with m ⩽ 0 are lifted by varying δ. We see in Fig. 1 that at δ = 0 the energy levels converge to the corresponding Landau levels with n = 0 , 1 , and the eigenenergies of the GQD tend to decrease with increasing δ. Especially for
B i
= 0 , one can find from the calculation that the previous results are obtained again [12]. However, when the magnetic field inside the dot is present, i.e.
B i
≠ 0 , the energy levels tend to contract each other, and the level crossings disappear, as shown in Fig. 1(b). This is due to the fact that the inner magnetic field
B i
can offset the effect of the missing of magnetic flux, and thus the breaking of the Landau level degeneracy is not more pronounced than in Fig. 1(a). Therefore, the magnetic field inside the dot not only modifies the energy spectrum of the GQD, but can also affect the lifting of the Landau level degeneracy.

The energies of the eigenstates with m ⩽ 0 as functions of the inner magnetic field
B i
(
B i
> 0 ) are drawn in Fig. 2 (a) at B = 4 T and R = 50 nm . As can be seen from Fig. 2(a), for small values of
B i
, there is an obvious lifting of the Landau level degeneracy. However, as
B i
increases, the energy levels contract gradually and finally, at
B i
= 4 , the energy levels converge to the corresponding Landau levels. It is due to the fact that the ( n , m < 0 ) states are localized in the deeper region of the dot with increase of
B i
, where the magnetic field is uniform, and thus their energies approach the Landau levels. In Fig. 2(b), we also present the dependence of some low-lying energy levels on the direction of the magnetic fields inside and outside the dot. In particular, when the inner magnetic field
B i
has a different sign from the outer magnetic field B, the spectrum shows some different features. This is because for
B i
< 0 , when |
B i
| is increased, the states for m < 0 are located farther away from the dot referred to in Eq. (6), and finally the energy levels converge to the Landau level induced by the fixed outer magnetic field B.

In Fig. 3 , we show the dependence of the eigenenergies of the GQD on angular quantum number m at B = 4 T and R = 50 nm with or without the inner magnetic field
B i
. It is known from Eq. (6) that if the condition of |
m ˜
1
| < δ is satisfied, the minimum value of
V ˜
( r ) always occurs at r = R for the case of
B i
= 0 , and then the states are confined on the boundary of the dot; while for |
m ˜
1
| > δ , the minima of
V ˜
( r ) are located outside the dot, especially for m ≪ 0 the states are distributed far from the dot, and hence their energies are very close to the Landau levels. Also, we notice that the energies of the states near the dot edge deviate prominently from the Landau levels due to the missing of magnetic flux, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Furthermore, in the case of
B i
≠ 0 , we can see from Fig. 3(b) that the energy levels which deviate from the Landau levels are lifted up, and the energies of some states with m > 0 are almost independent of m. This is because the inner magnetic field
B i
can further accumulate the magnetic confinement, and then more states tend to distribute within the dot, whereas some states with m > 0 are localized on the boundary region of the dot and form the so-called magnetic edge states. This behavior is in a sharp contrast to the case of
B i
= 0 .

In order to show further the magnetic confinement, we plot in Fig. 4 the probability density of the Dirac electron in graphene at B = 20 T with or without the inner magnetic field
B i
. The results indicate that with a higher magnetic field inside the GQD, more states with m < 0 are confined in the deeper region of the dot, whereas the states with m > 0 are strongly localized along the boundary of the dot and form the obvious edge states. This happens because although the magnetic barrier becomes smaller with increasing
B i
, the magnetic confinement induced by
B i
becomes stronger, and thus the states with m < 0 are confined in the deeper region of the dot. Especially when
B i
⩾ B , the energy levels of the ( n , m < 0 ) states can converge to the Landau levels defined by
B i
, as can be also seen from Fig. 2. The appearance of the edge states is a valid evidence of confining massless Dirac electrons in a finite region in graphene. On the other hand, these confined states can also be regarded as the ensemble average of classical orbits, including the cyclotron motion with magnetic field and the straight line paths without magnetic field. Thus, Fig. 4 helps to understand the correspondence between the quantum eigenstates and the classical motions.

In summary, we have theoretically investigated the magnetically confined states of massless Dirac fermions in a graphene quantum dot formed by the inhomogeneous magnetic fields. The numerical results have indicated that the degeneracy of the relativistic Landau level with negative angular momenta can be lifted by varying the missing of magnetic flux in the dot, and the magnetic field inside the dot can obviously modify the energy spectrum of the system and influence the lifting of the Landau level degeneracy. In addition, we have shown the probability density distribution of the Dirac electron in graphene sheet with or without the magnetic field inside the dot. It has been found that the states are more localized within the dot and around its boundary with than without the magnetic field inside the dot.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (Grant Nos. 10674058, 60876065) and the State Key Program for Basic Research (Grant Nos. 2006CB921803, 2009CB929504) of China.
